coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of biofunctional carbon quantum dots for bacterial labeling
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Weng, Cheng-I
Chang, Huan-Tsung
Lin, Chia-Hua
Shen, Yu-Wei
Unnikrishnan, Binesh
Li, Yu-Jia
Huang, Chih-Ching
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we used a simple one-step dry heating method to synthesize mannose-modified fluorescent carbon quantum dots (Man-CQDs) from solid ammonium citrate and mannose, and successfully applied for labeling Escherichia coli. The highly soluble Man-CQDs had an average particle diameter of 3.1±1.2nm and exhibited a quantum yield of 9.8% at excitation and emission wavelengths of 365 and 450nm, respectively. The fluorescent Man-CQDs could selectively bind to the FimH lectin unit in the flagella of the wild-type 1 E. coli K12 strain. We optimized the labeling efficiency of the Man-CQDs by controlling the ratio of ammonium citrate to mannose during their synthesis. The specific binding of the mannose units to E. coli allowed quantitative detection of the bacteria at levels down to 450 colony forming units mL−1 in lab samples, and facilitate the application of the Man-CQDs for bacterial analyses of real samples (tap water, apple juice, human urine). The synthesis of our Man-CQDs, their labeling, and their use in the detection of bacteria were all simple, inexpensive and efficient processes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Mannose
Bacterial labeling
Lectin
E. coli

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Specificity and sensitivity of Man-CQDs for the detection of E. coli

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (B) (a) UV–vis absorption and (b) fluorescence spectra of as-prepared Man-CQDs (0.01×) in 0.1M NaOH solution. Inset to (A): HRTEM image of Man-CQDs. Inset to (B): Photographs of the Man-CQD solution before and after excitation with a UV lamp (365nm). Fluorescence spectrum in (b) was measured with excitation at 365nm. The absorbance (Abs) and fluorescence (I F) intensities are plotted in arbitrary units.

Fluorescence spectra of E. coli (1.0×108 CFUmL−1) after labeling with (A) Man-CQDs (0.1×) and (B) CQDs (0.1×) in PBS. Inset: Photographic images of pellets of (a) Man-CQDs/E. coli and (b) CQDs/E. coli complexes under illumination from a UV lamp (365nm).

Fluorescence spectra of Man-CQDs (0.01×) used as probes for the detection of E. coli (1.0×102–1.0×108 CFUmL−1). Inset: Plot of fluorescence intensity at 450nm (I F450) with respect to E. coli concentration; error bars represent standard deviations from three repeated experiments. Other conditions were the same as those described in Fig. 2.

Fluorescence intensities (at 450nm) of solutions of Man-CQDs (0.01×) and E. coli in (A) tap water, (B) apple juice, and (C) human urine samples. The samples (10-fold diluted) were spiked with E. coli at concentrations from 0 to 1.0×108 CFUmL−1. Error bars represent standard deviations from four repeated experiments. Other conditions were the same as those described in Fig. 3.
Cartoon representations of (A) the synthesis of mannose-modified carbon quantum dots (Man-CQDs) from ammonium citrate and mannose and (B) their use in the selective labeling of E. coli.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of biofunctional carbon quantum dots for bacterial labeling

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Kaohsiung Medical University, Kaohsiung 80708, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Kaohsiung Medical University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Bioscience and Biotechnology, National Taiwan Ocean University, 2, Pei-Ning Road, Keelung 20224, Taiwan. Fax: +886 2 2462 2320.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we used a simple one-step dry heating method to synthesize mannose-modified fluorescent carbon quantum dots (Man-CQDs) from solid ammonium citrate and mannose, and successfully applied for labeling Escherichia coli. The highly soluble Man-CQDs had an average particle diameter of 3.1±1.2nm and exhibited a quantum yield of 9.8% at excitation and emission wavelengths of 365 and 450nm, respectively. The fluorescent Man-CQDs could selectively bind to the FimH lectin unit in the flagella of the wild-type 1 E. coli K12 strain. We optimized the labeling efficiency of the Man-CQDs by controlling the ratio of ammonium citrate to mannose during their synthesis. The specific binding of the mannose units to E. coli allowed quantitative detection of the bacteria at levels down to 450 colony forming units mL−1 in lab samples, and facilitate the application of the Man-CQDs for bacterial analyses of real samples (tap water, apple juice, human urine). The synthesis of our Man-CQDs, their labeling, and their use in the detection of bacteria were all simple, inexpensive and efficient processes.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent nanomaterials (e.g., semiconductor quantum dots, dye-doped polymers, silica nanoparticles, metallic nanoclusters) have attracted much attention for their attractive properties relative to those of conventional organic dyes, with great potential for use in a variety of applications, from nanosensing to bioimaging (Ruedas–Rama et al., 2012; Zhang and Wang, 2014; Knopp et al., 2009; Li and Zhu, 2013). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have recently emerged as important nanomaterials for sensing, photocatalysis and photoelectronic applications because of their attractive fluorescence properties, including high quantum yields (QYs), photostability and excitation-dependent emissions (Esteves da Silva and Gonçalves, 2011; Li et al., 2012a; Demchenko and Dekaliuk, 2013; Luo et al., 2014; Baker and Baker, 2010; Dong et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2014a; Pan et al., 2010). CQDs generally refer to small carbon nanoparticles (<10nm) with appropriate crystallization in their cores and surfaces passivated by organic molecules resulting in bright and colorful fluorescence emissions (Anilkumar et al., 2013; Bourlinos et al., 2012). Compared with heavy metal (e.g., Hg, Cd, Pb)-based semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) (Soenen et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2012), CQDs exhibit relatively high chemical and optical stabilities, superior water dispersibility, good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity (Esteves da Silva and Gonçalves, 2011; Li et al., 2012a; Demchenko and Dekaliuk, 2013; Luo et al., 2014; Baker and Baker, 2010; Dong et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2014a; Pan et al., 2010; Anilkumar et al., 2013; Bourlinos et al., 2012). These unique features make CQDs promising nanomaterials for use as optical nanoprobes in bio-imaging applications. Moreover, CQDs can be combined with metal, metal oxide, polymer, silica nanoparticles and graphene oxide nanosheets to construct fluorescent nanohybrids presenting multiple functionalities (Liu et al., 2014a; Yu et al., 2014; Qin et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2014; Zong et al., 2013; Choi et al., 2013).

Following the discovery of the production of CQDs through laser ablation of carbon targets (Sun et al., 2006), several methods—both top-down (e.g., arc-discharge, electrochemical exfoliation, confined combustion, chemical oxidation, ultrasonic synthesis) and bottom-up (e.g., thermal decomposition or oxidation, microwave-assisted pyrolysis, hydrothermal carbonization)—have been developed for their synthesis (Qiao et al., 2010; Rahy et al., 2012; Sahu et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2011a, 2011b; Yang et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2014b). Nevertheless, most of these developed methods are unsatisfactory for practical applications because of their low yields and the need for expensive equipment and/or complicated procedures. Moreover, the resulting CQDs require further passivation and modification to render their surfaces hydrophilic and to present various functionalities. Thus, the challenge remains to devise simple methods for the synthesis of self-passivated and self-functionalized CQDs.

Herein, we report a new, simple and straightforward preparative route, a one-step reaction, toward biofunctional CQDs for labeling of bacteria ( Scheme. 1). We developed a simple bottom-up method to prepare mannose-modified CQDs (Man-CQDs) through carbonization of ammonium citrate in solid form in the presence of mannose. The resulting N-doped, carbon-rich, highly photoluminescent Man-CQDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence, a short fluorescence lifetime and excellent photostability. We then employed our as-prepared Man-CQDs for the selective labeling of Escherichia coli (E. coli) without the need for any further surface modification.

Ammonium citrate and mannose were purchased from Acros (Geel, Belgium). Sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid, sodium chloride, potassium chloride, magnesium chloride, calcium chloride, Luria Bertani (LB) Broth Lennox (10gL−1 tryptone, 5gL−1 yeast extract, 5gL−1 NaCl), tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris), lactose, glucose, fructose, sucrose, and agarose were purchased from Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI). E. coli K12 (BCRC 12438), Staphylococcus aureus (BCRC 10780), Proteus vulgaris (BCRC 17528), and Bacillus subtilis (BCRC 11594) were purchased from the Bioresource Collection and Research Center (BCRC, Hsinchu, Taiwan).

A mixture of ammonium citrate (0.1g) and mannose (0.005g) in a 10-mL beaker was placed in an oven and heated at a temperature of 180°C for 2h. The black residue that formed was cooled to room temperature and then dissolved in sodium hydroxide solution (100mM, 10mL) in a beaker. This solution was sonicated for 1h and then centrifuged at a relative centrifugal force (RCF) of 35,000g for 1h. The Man-CQD solution (5mL) was dialyzed in deionized water (ca. 1L) through a dialysis membrane (MWCO=1.0 kD; Float-A-Lyzer G2, Spectrum Laboratories, Rancho Dominguez, CA) for 5h, with the water replaced with ultrapure water every 60min. The solution of purified Man-CQDs was stored at 4°C when not in use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the Man-CQDs were obtained using a Tecnai 20 G2 S-Twin transmission electron microscope (Philips/FEI, Hillsboro, OR); their average diameters were determined to be 3.1±1.2nm (from 100 counts). Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra of solutions of as-prepared Man-CQDs were recorded using a monochromatic microplate spectrophotometer (Synergy 4 Multi-Mode; Biotek Instruments, Winooski, VT). The fluorescence QY of the Man-CQDs was determined by comparison with that of quinine (QY=53% in 0.1M H2SO4). The zeta potentials (ζ) of the Man-CQDs were assessed using a Zetasizer (Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK). Samples for X-ray diffraction (XRD) were prepared by depositing the Man-CQDs on a Si(100) wafer; XRD measurements were performed at room temperature using a Rigaku 18-kW rotating anode source X-ray diffractometer (operated at 50kV and 100mA) with the Cu Ka1 line (λ=1.54Å).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an ES-CALAB 250 spectrometer (VG Scientific, East Grinstead, UK) with Al Kα X-ray radiation as the X-ray source for excitation. Binding energies were corrected using the C 1s peak at 284.6eV as the standard. The fluorescence lifetime of the Man-CQDs was recorded using a photo-counting PicoHarp 300 system (PicoPicoQuant, Berlin, Germany), with a diode laser emitting at 375nm (FluoTime 300) as the light source. An Agilent Cary 640 FT-IR spectrometer (Santa Clara, CA) was used to identify the functional groups present in the Man-CQDs. The possible components of the Man-CQDs were analyzed through laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight ionization mass spectrometry (LDI-TOF MS). Mass spectrometry experiments were performed in the reflectron positive-ion mode using an AutoflexIII LDI-TOF MS (Bruker Daltonics, Bremen, Germany). The samples were irradiated using pulsed laser irradiation (Nd:YAG, 355nm, 100Hz; pulse width: 6ns). A total of 500 pulsed laser shots were applied to accumulate signals from five MALDI target positions under laser irradiation of 2.48×104 Wcm−2.

Bacterial culturing and sample handling were performed in a sterile clean room. Each piece of apparatus was sterilized in an autoclave at high temperature and pressure. E. coli K12, P. vulgaris, S. aureus, and B. subtilis were grown separately in LB media [containing bacto-tryptone (2.5g), bacto-yeast extract (1.25g), and NaCl (1.25g) in deionized water (250mL)]. A single colony of each strain was lifted from LB agar plates and inoculated in LB media (10mL). The cultures were grown at 37°C with shaking (200rpm) until the absorbance at 600nm (OD600) reached 1.0 (optical path length: 1.0cm). A portion of each cell mixture (1.0mL) was centrifuged (RCF 3000g, 10min, 25°C) and washed twice with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; 10mM Na2HPO4, 140mM NaCl, 2.70mM KCl; adjusted to pH 7.4 using HCl; 1mL) prior to further use. The cells were diluted to a concentration of 1.0×103–1.0×108 colony forming units (CFU) mL−1 and then they were incubated with Man-CQDs (0.01×) for 1h under gentle shaking at room temperature. The concentration of as-prepared Man-CQDs is denoted herein as 1×. The mixtures were then centrifuged (10,000g, 20min) and washed with PBS. The final bacteria pellets were resuspended in PBS and transferred separately into 96-well microtiter plates; their fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Synergy 4 multi-mode microplate spectrophotometer, with excitation at a wavelength of 365nm.

Aliquots (1.0mL) of diluted (10%, w/w) local tap water, apple juice (purchased from a traditional supermarket in Taipei, Taiwan), and human urine (obtained from a male adult, 22 years old) were incubated with desired concentrations of E. coli (from 0 to 1×108 CFUmL−1). The E. coli–spiked samples were analyzed using both the plate-count method and our Man-CQD-based assay, as described above for bacterial detection.

We prepared the Man-CQDs by directly pyrolyzing ammonium citrate in the presence of mannose. In a typical synthesis, ammonium citrate (0.1g) and mannose (0.005g) were mixed and manually ground with an agate mortar and pestle for 5min. The resulting powder was placed in a 5-mL beaker and heated at 180°C for 2h in a heating furnace (Dong et al., 2012). The yield of the black Man-CQD product was approximately 67%. When the black solid was dissolved in NaOH solution (10mL), the concentration of the purified Man-CQDs was denoted as 1× (ca. 7.0mgmL−1). The 0.01× Man-CQD solution had a brown color; it exhibited strong blue fluorescence (inset to Fig. 1B) when excited with a UV lamp (365nm). The as-prepared Man-CQDs were uniform, monodisperse spheres having a mean diameter (determined from 100 counts) of 3.5±1.2nm (Fig. 1A). The lattice structure of the Man-CQDs (inset to Fig. 1A) featured a d-spacing of 0.34nm, in good agreement with the XRD pattern (002 plane; 2θ=26.6°) as displayed in Fig. S1A (Supplementary data) (Wang et al., 2013). Fig. 1B (curve b) reveals the bright blue fluorescence (450nm) of the Man-CQDs under an excitation wavelength of 365nm, attributable to Π–Π⁎ transitions (Wang et al., 2014b). We determined the QY of the Man-CQDs to be 9.8% [from comparison with quinine (QY 53%; in 0.1M H2SO4)]. This QY is comparable to those of other N-doped CQDs described in previous reports (Esteves da Silva and Gonçalves, 2011; Li et al., 2012a; Demchenko and Dekaliuk, 2013; Luo et al., 2014; Baker and Baker, 2010; Dong et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2014a; Pan et al., 2010; Anilkumar et al., 2013; Bourlinos et al., 2012; Qiao et al., 2010; Rahy et al., 2012; Sahu et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2011a, 2011b; Yang et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2014b). XPS measurements confirmed the presence of O and N atoms in the Man-CQDs (Fig. S1B, Supplementary data). We determined the fluorescence lifetime (τ 1/τ 2) of the Man-CQDs to be 12.11 (54%)/4.21 (46%) ns, with excitation and emission at wavelengths of 365 and 450nm, respectively, as displayed in Fig. S2A (Supplementary data). Similar to previous reports (Nie et al., 2014; Kozák et al., 2013; Li et al., 2012b), the emission wavelength of our Man-CQDs depends on the excitation wavelength, with the maximum excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength at 360 and 440nm, respectively (Fig. S2B, Supplementary data). Usually, excitation-dependent fluorescence of CQDs reflects the presence of QDs possessing different hybridized compositions and structures, various emissive trap sizes and a distribution of different surface states (Zhang et al., 2013). Beyond the excitation-dependent emission, we further investigated the fluorescence properties of our Man-CQDs under different conditions. The emission behavior of the Man-CQDs was independent of the pH and ionic strength of the medium (Fig. S3A and S3B, Supplementary data). In addition, no significant photobleaching of the Man-CQDs occurred during continuous excitation at 365nm (2.3mWcm−2) for 2h (Fig. S3C, Supplementary data), suggesting possible suitability in bioimaging applications. The Man-CQDs powder has good stability for over six months. It showed consistent results even after six months of storage at 4°C in air-tight closed vials.

Because our Man-CQDs had a high QY (9.8%), photostability and functionality (mannose ligands), we used them for selective labeling of an E. coli strain. FimH lectin units in the wild-type 1 pili on the surface of E. coli are responsible for the mannose- and mannoside-binding activities of the bacteria (Krogfelt et al., 1990). In this study, we tested an E. coli strain K12 that expresses wild-type 1 pili. After incubating bacterial suspensions of E. coli (1×108 CFUmL−1) with the Man-CQDs (0.1×) or CQDs (0.1×) in phosphate buffered saline [PBS; 10mM sodium phosphate (pH 7.4), 140mM NaCl, 2.70mM KCl; 1.0mL] at 25°C for 1h, we centrifuged the suspensions, washed the resulting bacteria pellets once with PBS and then resuspended them in PBS (1.0mL). Also, we synthesized CQDs without mannose functionalization, by the same method as that of Man-CQDs, in the absence of mannose during the pyrolysis of ammonium citrate.

The fluorescence properties (QY, emission wavelength, lifetime) of the bare CQDs and the Man-CQDs were almost identical (data not shown), revealing that the core structures of the two types of CQDs were very similar. As expected, only the Man-CQDs functioned as labels on the E. coli (curve A in Fig. 2). We recorded optical images of the E. coli before and after treatment with the Man-CQDs. In addition to bright-field images, Fig. S4 (Supplementary data) displays blue, green and red bacterial images when excited with long-wavelength UV light (360–380nm), blue light (460–480nm) and green light (510–530nm), respectively, further confirming that the Man-CQDs selectively targeted the E. coli. In a control experiment (in the absence of the Man-CQDs), the E. coli did not display any autofluorescence upon excitation (data not shown). The FimH located at the tip of type 1 pili present in E. coli has very specific and strong interaction with d-mannose, which is highly stable. The Man-CQDs labeled E. coli exhibited 95% performance after 48h of labeling. We used laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight ionization mass spectrometry (LDI-TOF MS) to determine the surface components of the purified Man-CQDs. Signals at m/z 203.094 and 220.092 correspond to [mannose+Na]+ and [mannose+K]+ ions in the positive mode, confirming the existence of mannose units on the surfaces of the CQDs (Fig. S5B, Supplementary data). In contrast, no such signals were observed in the spectrum of the unmodified CQDs (Fig. S5A). We used FT-IR spectroscopy to identify the surface functional groups on the CQDs and Man-CQDs (Fig. S6, Supplementary data). Relative to the unmodified CQDs, we observed a higher degree of C–O stretching vibrations (1074cm−1) for the Man-CQDs, further confirming the conjugation of mannose units on the surfaces of the latter species. The presence of additional O–C units was also confirmed in the O 1s spectra from XPS measurements (Fig. S1Bb, Supplementary data). We tested the possibility of capping mannose onto CQDs through simple mixing of the unmodified CQDs and mannose (0–500mM) in aqueous solutions, but the resulting CQDs were not able to label E. coli (data not shown). Although we do not fully understand how the mannose units modified the CQDs during the synthesis of the Man-CQDs, we suggest that the mannose molecules were directly anchored (physically adsorbed) onto the soft surfaces of the CQDs upon the pyrolysis of ammonium citrate. We cannot, however, exclude the possibility of some degree of covalent bonding of mannose units to the CQDs occurring through dehydration reactions during the carbonization of ammonium citrate (Wu et al., 2014; Lai et al., 2012; Krysmann et al., 2012; Xiao et al., 2013).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and sensitivity of Man-CQDs for the detection of E. coli
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the specificity of our Man-CQDs toward E. coli, we developed binding inhibition assays based on the decrease in binding efficiency between E. coli (108 CFUmL−1) and the Man-CQDs (0.01×) in the presence of mannose, glucose, fructose, sucrose, or lactose (250mM) in PBS. The binding of the Man-CQDs to E. coli was inhibited to a high degree in the presence of 250mM mannose (ca. 90% decrease), whereas the presence of glucose (250mM) had only a slight effect (ca. 8% decrease) (data not shown), revealing that we could attribute the binding of the Man-CQDs to E. coli to specific interactions between the mannose units and the FimH proteins of E. coli. In addition, labeling test performed using a series of carbohydrate-conjugated CQDs [Man-CQDs, glucose-conjugated CQDs (Glu-CQDs), fructose-conjugated CQDs (Fru–CQDs), sucrose-conjugated CQDs (Suc-CQDs), lactose-conjugated CQDs (Lac-CQDs)] revealed that only the Man-CQDs and Glu-CQDs could bind to E. coli (Fig. S7, Supplementary data). The syntheses of these other carbohydrate-conjugated CQDs were performed using the same procedure as that for the Man-CQDs. The fluorescence properties of these carbohydrate-conjugated CQDs were almost the same (data not shown). Relative to the strong interactions between mannose and FimH, the weaker binding affinity between glucose and FimH resulted in lower fluorescence intensity when using the Glu–CQDs to label E. coli than that of the Man-CQDs (Fig. S7). We also investigated the interactions of the Man-CQDs with different bacteria: E. coli, P. vulgaris, S. aureus and B. subtilis (Fig. S8, Supplementary data). The multivalent binding between the Man-CQDs and the FimH proteins on the tips of the type 1 fimbriae of E. coli resulted in the Man-CQDs binding strongly and selectively only to E. coli (Huang et al., 2009). The tolerance of Man-CQDs probe for E. coli ions toward these bacteria was greater than 1000-fold. In the presence of P. vulgaris, S. aureus, or B. subtilis (105 CFUmL−1), negative errors were smaller than 5% when we used the Man-CQDs probe to detect 103 CFUmL−1 of E. coli (data not shown). The main factor that determines the selectivity or our method is the specific binding of the FimH lectin with the mannose molecules.

Next, we compared the labeling efficiency of a series of Man-CQDs for E. coli. As displayed in Fig. S9 (Supplementary data), the Man-CQDs prepared from 0.1g of ammonium citrate and 0.005g of mannose provided the best labeling efficiency, presumably because of its high content of surface mannose ligands and its strong fluorescence. The weaker labeling efficiencies of the CQDs prepared from 0.1g of ammonium citrate and greater than 0.010g of mannose resulted from their weaker fluorescence (Fig. S10, Supplementary data); indeed, the fluorescence intensities of the Man-CQDs prepared from 0.1g ammonium citrate/0.02g mannose and from 0.1g ammonium citrate/0.2g mannose were 0.66- and 0.37-fold lower than that of the Man-CQDs prepared from 0.1g ammonium citrate/0.005g mannose.

Fig. 3 reveals that the fluorescence increased upon increasing the concentration of E. coli after labeling with the optimized Man-CQDs (prepared from 0.1g of ammonium citrate and 0.005g of mannose), with a limit of detection (LOD; at an S/N ratio of 3) of E. coli of approximately 450CFUmL−1. Also, the reproducibility of the method has been evaluated from three different measurements (as can be seen from the inset of Fig. 3) and found to exhibit relative standard deviation (RSD) values less than 10% for the E. coli concentration range from 103 to 108 CFUmL−1. The LOD is comparable with or better than those obtained using other nanomaterial-based optical bacterial sensors (Duan et al., 2013; Lesniewski et al., 2014; Tang et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2012; Chung et al., 2011; Kim and Son, 2010). Notably, however, the preparation of our Man-CQDs is relatively simple, inexpensive and amenable to scale-up. Although cell plating and culture-based analyses can provide greater sensitivity for the detection of bacteria, they are very time-consuming (>24h), labor-intensive and display low selectivity (Davis, 2014; Lazcka, 2007).

To examine the feasibility of using our new nanoprobe in practical applications, we used it to detect E. coli in various matrices, including tap water, apple juice and human urine. In these cases, a desired amount of bacteria (from 0 to 1×108 CFUmL−1) was introduced into the 10-fold diluted matrices and incubated for 1h. Subsequently, the E. coli was detected using both our Man-CQD probe and an agar plate count method. Neither system could detect the presence of E. coli in the unspiked tap water, apple juice and urine samples (Fig. S11, Supplementary data). For the tap water, apple juice and human urine samples spiked with E. coli at concentrations from 1×103 to 1×108 CFUmL−1, we observed similar relationships between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of E. coli spiked into the samples ( Fig. 4). The minimum detectable concentration of E. coli in these three samples when using our probe was approximately 103 CFUmL−1. These results suggest that our rapid, cheap and simple Man-CQD probe has great potential for use in the rapid screening of E. coli in real samples.

We have developed a simple method for the synthesis of mannose-functionalized CQDs for the labeling and detection of E. coli. To the best of our knowledge, our preparation of Man-CQDs is the first example of the one-step synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots presenting functionality for bacterial labeling. We believe that the mannose units were anchored on the surfaces of the CQDs during the pyrolysis of ammonium citrate. The physical properties of the as-synthesized Man-CQDs were independent of the ionic strength and pH and they displayed ultrahigh photostability, suggesting that they would be highly useful in many bioimaging applications. The selective binding of mannose to the lectin units of E. coli enabled our fluorescent Man-CQDs to perform specific labeling of E. coli. The Man-CQDs displayed their maximum labeling efficiency when the ratio of ammonium citrate to mannose during the synthesis was 20:1 (w/w). We applied our Man-CQD as label for fluorescence detection method to detect E. coli at concentrations as low as 103 CFUmL−1 in real samples (tap water, apple juice, human urine), revealing its potential for use in real-life applications. Compared with other detection methods using functional nanomaterials (Duan et al., 2013; Lesniewski et al., 2014; Tang et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2012; Chung et al., 2011; Kim and Son, 2010), our Man-CQD probe is simple, rapid and highly selective. The one-pot synthesis of functional CQDs developed in this study suggests a new approach for the synthesis of various functional CQD probes for the detection of pathogens and tumor cells and for animal imaging. However, the method needs further improvement in order to increase the sensitivity for practical use in the detection of E. coli in very low concentrations in real samples. Future efforts could be focused on two-step synthesis of Man-CQDs in solid form (preparation of CQDs and then anchor of mannose ligands on their surfaces) to increase the photoluminescence quantum efficiency and mannose density on the surfaces of CODs, to improve the sensitivity. Moreover, further modification of the Man-CQDs with magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs; e.g., iron oxide, nickel oxide, cobalt pxide nanoparticles) will improve multivalent binding between the Man-CQDs/MNPs nanocomposites and E. coli and achieve a simple one-step magnetic separation/labeling process for detection of E. coli.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology for Taiwan under contract NSC 101-2628-M-019-001-MY3, 102-2113-M-019-001-MY3 and 102-2627-M-019-001-MY3.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2014.12.028.
